title: Courtroom-Style Multi-Agent Debate with Progressive RAG and Role-Switching for Controversial Claim Verification
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) remain unreliable for high-stakes claim verification due to hallucinations and shallow reasoning. While retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and multi-agent debate (MAD) address this, they are limited by one-pass retrieval and unstructured debate dynamics. We propose a courtroom-style multi-agent framework, PROClaim, that reformulates verification as a structured, adversarial deliberation. Our approach integrates specialized roles (e.g., Plaintiff, Defense, Judge) with Progressive RAG (P-RAG) to dynamically expand and refine the evidence pool during the debate. Furthermore, we employ evidence negotiation, self-reflection, and heterogeneous multi-judge aggregation to enforce calibration, robustness, and diversity. In zero-shot evaluations on the Check-COVID benchmark, PROClaim achieves 81.7% accuracy, outperforming standard multi-agent debate by 10.0 percentage points, with P-RAG driving the primary performance gains (+7.5 pp). We ultimately demonstrate that structural deliberation and model heterogeneity effectively mitigate systematic biases, providing a robust foundation for reliable claim verification. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/mnc13/PROClaim.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
LLMs have demonstrated strong zero-shot performance on reasoning-intensive tasks, yet their reliability in high-stakes domains, such as claim verification, remains limited. Despite fluent generation, LLMs frequently exhibit hallucinations, shallow reasoning, and overconfident predictions when evaluating evidence-dependent claims (Huang et al. , 2025 ) . RAG partially addresses these issues by grounding responses in external corpora (Lewis et al. , 2020 ) . Still, standard pipelines rely on static, single-pass retrieval (Gao et al. , 2024 ) and lack mechanisms for iterative reasoning, often resulting in incomplete or biased conclusions.

To improve reliability, recent work has explored Multi-Agent Debate (MAD), where multiple LLM instances iteratively argue and refine answers (Du et al. , 2024 ; Liang et al. , 2024 ; Han et al. , 2025 ) . While promising, prior studies show that unstructured debate often leads to premature convergence, shared bias reinforcement, and limited evidence exploration (Smit et al. , 2023 ; Wu et al. , 2025 ) . In particular, agent agreement is frequently misinterpreted as correctness, even when grounded in insufficient or biased evidence.

In this work, we propose a courtroom-style multi-agent deliberation framework PROClaim ( P rogressive R etrieval O rchestrated multi-agent framework for Claim verification) that reformulates claim verification as a structured, adversarial reasoning process. Inspired by legal systems and recent agent-based simulations (Chen et al. , 2025 ) , our approach introduces explicit roles (Plaintiff, Defense, Judge, Critic, and Expert Witness), evidence admission protocols, and multi-stage deliberation. Central to the framework is Progressive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (P-RAG), which performs iterative, query-adaptive retrieval to continuously expand and refine the evidence pool during debate.

Contributions. We present a unified framework where structured deliberation outperforms standard multi-agent debate, achieving a +10.0 pp accuracy gain and validating a courtroom-style architecture. Dynamic retrieval via P-RAG is the primary driver, adding 7.5 points while preventing evidence stagnation. We further show that model diversity is crucial: heterogeneous LLMs exhibit complementary errors that offset each other, outperforming any single model. PROClaim also demonstrates strong zero-shot, domain-agnostic performance. Beyond gains, our analysis reveals deeper insights into multi-agent reasoning: • Breaking the epistemic bubble , removing dynamic retrieval (P-RAG) increases inter-judge agreement ( κ ¯ = 0.468 → 0.599 \bar{\kappa}=0.468\rightarrow 0.599 ) while reducing accuracy by 7.5 pp , revealing confident convergence on incorrect conclusions. • Acting as a logic-level lie detector , incorrect predictions exhibit unstable reasoning trajectories with oscillating self-reflection scores, showing that reasoning dynamics are more informative than final confidence . • Enabling synthetic wisdom through diversity , heterogeneous LLMs exhibit complementary error profiles ( e.g. , over-refutation vs. cautious abstention) whose combination yields a 3.3 pp accuracy gain over single-judge panels. • Serving as an economic governor , self-reflection reduces debate rounds by 29% (7.06 → \rightarrow 5.47) and token usage by 17% while maintaining accuracy within 0.8 pp . • Demonstrating the butterfly effect of argument framing , small biases in premise decomposition propagate and significantly impact outcomes , as seen in Run-2 where κ G ​ T \kappa_{GT} drops to 0.384 (down from 0.423 in Run-0) despite stable inter-judge κ \kappa . • Revealing structural negativity bias , LLM judges over-produce REFUTE verdicts relative to ground truth and converge faster on such claims (0.2 rounds in primary and 0.3 in role-switched debates), indicating inherent conservatism shaped by their training data.

Together, these findings reframe LLM reasoning as a dynamic process, where reliability arises from the structure, diversity, and evolution of deliberation, not just final answers; see Appendices A for context with prior work. We provide our code and data in the following GitHub repository: https://github.com/mnc13/PROClaim .

sections.heading: 2Methodology
sections.text:
We propose a courtroom-inspired pipeline for zero-shot, evidence-grounded fact-checking of COVID-19-related claims. Each claim is treated as a legal case, where a Plaintiff Counsel argues for the claim, a Defense Counsel argues against it, and an independent judicial panel deliberates toward a verdict — imposing disciplined adversarial structure over the reasoning process rather than relying on single-pass LLM inference.
Figure 1 provides a high-level overview, with the full evaluation cycle formalised in Algorithm F .

sections.heading: 2.1Argument Mining
sections.text: Before retrieval, the raw claim is decomposed into atomic, independently testable premises (Hu et al. , 2025a ; Lawrence and Reed, 2017 ) . This serves two purposes: first, decomposing complex claims allows the retrieval system to cast a wider and more targeted net; second, the resulting premises act as an explicit checklist for scoring argument completeness during self-reflection and critic evaluation (Section 2.6 ). The output is a list of premise strings propagated to all downstream phases. Formally, given a claim c c , the miner produces a premise set 𝒫 = { p 1 , … , p k } \mathcal{P}=\{p_{1},\ldots,p_{k}\} where each p i p_{i} encodes a single verifiable proposition. The number of premises k k is not fixed, allowing the agent to produce as many non-redundant premises as the claim’s semantic content warrants.

sections.heading: 2.2Initial Evidence Retrieval
sections.text: Relevant evidence is retrieved from COVID-19 PubMed abstracts (2020–2024) corpus, constructed for this study, using dense vector embeddings to measure semantic similarity (Gao and Callan, 2021 ) . Claims and abstract chunks are mapped into a 384-dimensional embedding space using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 bi-encoder (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019 ) , and the most semantically similar chunks are selected as the seed evidence pool with source and year preserved for provenance. The detailed embedding normalization and similarity computation are provided in Appendix L .

sections.heading: 2.3Evidence Negotiation and Judicial Arbitration
sections.text:
Inspired by the pre-trial discovery phase in legal proceedings, this stage constructs an admissibility-weighted evidence pool before the debate begins, drawing on recent works (Jeong et al. , 2026 ; Hu et al. , 2025b ) , proceeding in four steps.

Premise-grounded shared retrieval: The FAISS index is queried once per decomposed premise, producing a shared pool of retrieved evidence.

Stance-conditioned retrieval: An LLM generates two distinct queries — one optimised to surface supporting evidence (for Plaintiff Counsel) and one for challenging evidence (for Defense Counsel) — yielding a proponent_pool and opponent_pool respectively. This asymmetric retrieval prevents the “Echo Chamber” effect common in Top-K RAG, where the engine retrieves only the most statistically frequent papers, often ignoring marginalized but critical counter-evidence (Wu and Saxena, 2025 ) , ensuring the evidence pool is adversarially balanced from Round 0.

Negotiation injection: Both sides are exposed to each other’s discovery pools via structured LLM prompts, simulating the disclosure and challenge process. Rather than merely retrieving more data, agents actively refine the search space based on their legal strategy — a form of metacognitive evidence curation.

Admissibility scoring: Each candidate is scored on two dimensions: Relevance r ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] r\in[0,1] , the degree to which the evidence pertains to the specific claim,
and Credibility c ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] c\in[0,1] , the scientific rigour and source quality.
The admissibility weight is w = r × c w=r\times c .
Items with w > 0.5 w>0.5 are admitted, 0.1 < w ≤ 0.5 0.1<w\leq 0.5 are flagged as disputed , and w ≤ 0.1 w\leq 0.1 are discarded.
Admitted items are ranked in descending order of w w and passed to the debate orchestrator (see Appendix H.2 ).

This mechanism operationalises a principle from both legal theory and fact-checking research: evidence quality and provenance matter beyond surface-level relevance, especially under conflicting sources (Ge et al. , 2025 ) . It mirrors the Daubert Standard (Legal Information Institute, 2024 ) used in real-world expert testimony, excluding “scientific hearsay” before it can pollute agent deliberation.

sections.heading: 2.4Progressive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (P-RAG)
sections.text:
Static, one-time retrieval is ill-suited to adversarial debate as new evidential gaps emerge as arguments evolve.
Unlike prior iterative approaches that query solely from the previous generation (Shao et al. , 2023 ; Trivedi et al. , 2023 ) , P-RAG concatenates three sources: (i) the last four messages of the rolling debate context, (ii) the agent’s self-identified evidential gap, and (iii) reflection-driven discovery needs from the prior round (Section 2.6 ) —into one prompt with the Judge refining each query before execution (see App. I for full logic and prompts).
To prevent redundant retrieval, each candidate item is scored against the existing pool:

novelty ⁡ ( d ) = 1 − max p ∈ 𝒫 ⁡ cos ⁡ ( e d , e p ) \operatorname{novelty}(d)=1-\max_{p\in\mathcal{P}}\cos(e_{d},\,e_{p}) (1) where 𝒫 \mathcal{P} is the current pool and e ⋅ e_{\cdot} denotes an L2-normalised embedding. Unlike global diversity metrics such as the Vendi Score (Rezaei and Dieng, 2025 ) , our novelty filter operates iteratively, rejecting near-duplicates at each round.

Only items with novelty ≥ 0.20 \geq 0.20 are admitted. This threshold is selected as a Redundancy-Noise Trade-off Heuristic; values below 0.15 allow near-duplicates, while values above 0.30 prematurely discard nuanced but critical evidence. Retrieval terminates early under the adaptive stopping criteria in Table 1 .

sections.heading: 2.5Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) Orchestration
2.6Self-Reflection and Critic Evaluation
sections.text:
Per-agent self-reflection. After each round, each counsel performs a structured self-critique (Madaan et al. , 2023 ; Shinn et al. , 2023 ) across three dimensions: logic l ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] l\in[0,1] (inferential validity), novelty n ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] n\in[0,1] (new information introduced), and rebuttal b ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] b\in[0,1] (engagement with the opposing side). Logical validity is weighted highest ( 0.4 0.4 ) as the core requirement for inferential integrity, with novelty and rebuttal balanced equally ( 0.3 0.3 each) to maintain adversarial accountability without compromising factual grounding.
They’re aggregated as: s ref = 0.4 ​ l + 0.3 ​ n + 0.3 ​ b , s_{\text{ref}}=0.4\,l+0.3\,n+0.3\,b, (2) and mapped to a symmetric confidence adjustment used in the final verdict (Section 2.8 ): δ ref = ( s ref − 0.5 ) × 0.6 ∈ [ − 0.30 , + 0.30 ] . \delta_{\text{ref}}=(s_{\text{ref}}-0.5)\times 0.6\;\in\;[-0.30,\;+0.30]. (3) The 0.6 0.6 multiplier scales the centered reflection score to a range of ± 0.30 \pm 0.30 . This allows the winning counsel’s self-critique to exert a significant, yet non-dominant, influence on the baseline consensus confidence ( c base ≈ 0.8 c_{\text{base}}\approx 0.8 ), maintaining the supremacy of the judicial panel’s verdict. The self-reflection output also surfaces a discovery need —a description of missing
evidence—which is included with the subsequent round’s P-RAG query. Independent Critic Agent. An independent Critic Agent, distinct from both debating agents, evaluates each round, leveraging prior findings that dedicated critics outperform self-critique (Li et al. , 2025b ) . It scores both sides on logic, evidence coverage, and rebuttal; identifying unaddressed premises from Section 2.1 ; providing actionable recommendations; and issuing the debate_resolved signal. Using an architecturally distinct model prevents both debaters from converging on a shared but potentially erroneous position.

sections.heading: 2.7Role-Switching Consistency Test
sections.text:
To probe whether arguments are genuinely evidence-grounded rather than position-anchored, we introduce a role-switching consistency test . Unlike prior work that uses role-switching to mitigate bias (Jin et al. , 2025 ) or elicit uncertainty (Liu et al. , 2025 ) , we employ it as a consistency diagnostic. After the primary debate, Plaintiff and Defense Counsel swap roles, with the orchestrator resetting all state and re-running the full debate under the switched configuration. This is analogous to requiring legal counsel to argue the opposing brief:
if arguments are evidence-driven, a capable agent should construct comparably strong arguments
from either position.

A separate LLM then analyses both transcripts, assessing whether the logic in the switched debate contradicts the agent’s original position and whether evidence is marshalled consistently or opportunistically, producing a consistency score for each agent. The score is incorporated into the final confidence weighting (Section 2.8 ).

sections.heading: 2.8Judicial Panel Evaluation and Final Verdict
sections.text:
The complete case record—both debate transcripts, admitted evidence, P-RAG retrieval histories, critic evaluations, and per-round self-reflection scores—is submitted to a three-member Judicial Panel of independent LLM judges, each assigned a different model to prevent correlated errors (Verga et al. , 2024 ) . Each judge independently evaluates the case through six stages: (1) case reconstruction, (2) evidence Strength, (3) argument Validity, (4) source Reliability, (5) discovery rigour and P-RAG query evolution, and (6) a final verdict of Supported , Not Supported , or Inconclusive .
The independent evaluations of the three judges are aggregated using a majority-vote mechanism to determine the final verdict. Appendix N . The confidence score is computed in two stages: Base confidence: c base = 0.8 ​ σ + 0.3 ​ q , c_{\text{base}}=0.8\,\sigma+0.3\,q, (4) where σ = winning_votes / total_votes \sigma=\texttt{winning\_votes}/\texttt{total\_votes} is the consensus strength and q = ( s ¯ ev + s ¯ val + s ¯ rel ) / 30 q=(\bar{s}_{\text{ev}}+\bar{s}_{\text{val}}+\bar{s}_{\text{rel}})/30 is the mean judge quality score. The 30 30 divisor normalizes the three 10-point qualitative metrics into a unit interval [ 0 , 1 ] [0,1] for mathematical consistency with σ \sigma . (Certainty buffer detailed in Appendix M.1.3 ) The weights were determined using a 5-fold cross-validation grid search (range [0.5, 0.9]) to minimize Expected Calibration Error (ECE) on a held-out development subset, following best practices for calibrating LLM-based evaluators (Gu et al. , 2025 ) . Our final test set calibration yielded a near-optimal ECE of 0.034 (see Appendix M.1 ), representing a 5x improvement over standard averaging ( W = 0.6 W=0.6 , ECE = 0.18 \text{ECE}=0.18 ). This high weighting ( W consensus = 0.8 W_{\text{consensus}}=0.8 ) is empirically justified as judge agreement proved the strongest predictor of accuracy.

Adjustment: c final = clamp ​ ( c base + δ rs + δ ref , 0 , 1 ) , c_{\text{final}}=\text{clamp}\bigl(c_{\text{base}}+\delta_{\text{rs}}+\delta_{\text{ref}},\;0,\;1\bigr), (5) where δ rs \delta_{\text{rs}} is the role-switch adjustment scalar (Details in Appendix M.2 ), and δ ref \delta_{\text{ref}} is the winning side’s final self-reflection adjustment. While the agent’s raw self-reflection score ranges from [ − 0.30 , + 0.30 ] [-0.30,+0.30] (Eq. 3 ), the framework applies a strict floor ( δ ref = max ⁡ ( − 0.15 , raw_score ) \delta_{\text{ref}}=\max(-0.15,\text{raw\_score}) ) to ensure that self-critiques mathematically refine—but do not unilaterally overturn—the established judicial consensus (see Appendix M.3 ). After clamping, if ≥ \geq 2/3 judges agree, confidence is lower-bounded at 0.10 to prevent near-zero scores under clear, majority agreement.

sections.heading: 3Experimental Setup
sections.text:
Dataset. To evaluate the framework’s capacity for adversarial resolution, we focus on the subset of the Check-COVID (Wang et al. , 2023a ) test set possessing definitive binary ground-truths (SUPPORT or REFUTE). This task formulation, which we term Adversarial Resolution of Hard-Binary Claims , ensures that the system is tested on its ability to navigate conflicting evidence toward a definitive truth, rather than defaulting to an uninformative ‘Not Enough Information’ label common in standard model outputs.

Retrieval Corpus. Retrieval is performed over a static corpus of COVID-19 abstracts from PubMed (2020–2024), constructed for this study. Abstracts are encoded using all-MiniLM-L6-v2 , a Sentence-BERT model (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019 ) , and indexed with FAISS (Douze et al. , 2025 ) . Source metadata, including PMID, title, journal, and publication year, are stored alongside each passage to support provenance tracking.

Configuration and Metrics. Table 2 summarizes the heterogeneous LLM assignment, where agent roles are partitioned across distinct architectures based on their functional capacity (deliberative vs. evaluative) and cognitive profile, utilizing moderate temperatures for adversarial advocacy and lower temperatures for rigorous judicial evaluation (details in Appendix B.3 ). Key retrieval hyperparameters are a novelty threshold τ = 0.20 \tau{=}0.20 , per-round top- k = 3 k{=}3 , and admissibility floor w > 0.5 w{>}0.5 ; full settings are in Appendix B.4 .

We evaluate classification performance (accuracy, macro F1), inter-judge reliability (Cohen’s κ \kappa , unanimity/split rates), confidence stability via the Kolmogorov–Smirnov statistic across rounds (App. E ), and efficiency (token usage, debate rounds, evidence pool size).

sections.heading: 4Results and Discussion
sections.text:
Table 3 reports Check-COVID performance across three independent runs, utilizing majority voting for aggregate results. Crucially, our framework enforces an adversarial ‘Burden of Refutation’. Mirroring legal systems where defendants are

formally presumed intact unless definitively proven otherwise, we evaluate claims based strictly on adversarial survival. If a claim withstands rigorous deliberation and the panel fails to reach a definitive Refute consensus—instead returning an Inconclusive verdict due to insufficient opposing evidence—the claim is formally classified as Supported. This strictly aligns our evaluation metrics with established courtroom logic (see Appendix C ).

sections.heading: 4.1Main Pipeline Performance
sections.text: This framework achieves a aggregate majority-vote accuracy of 81.7% across three independent runs, with an oracle ceiling of 95.8% confirming that near-optimal performance is structurally attainable under favourable initialisation. Inter-judge agreement remains stable across all runs (mean κ \kappa : 0.429, 0.549, 0.474), indicating coherent panel deliberation regardless of outcome. The observed variability across runs is primarily attributable to stochastic effects in sequential agent interactions. Run-1’s lower κ G ​ T \kappa_{GT} (0.372) does not translate to equivalent accuracy loss (0.817), as its errors remain class-balanced. Run-2, however, exhibits class-asymmetric degradation: retrieval bias introduced during stochastic premise decomposition suppresses REFUTE recall and propagates through deliberation, dropping κ G ​ T \kappa_{GT} to 0.384 while inter-judge κ \kappa holds stable at 0.474 — confirming the panel converged confidently on systematically biased verdicts rather than breaking down structurally.

sections.heading: 4.2Debate Dynamics and Adaptive Stopping
sections.text:
Termination distribution: Figure 2(a) shows that most debates self-terminate through reflection-driven signals: Reflection Plateau and Judicial Signal. Reflection Plateau enforces adaptive halting once marginal argumentative gains diminish, mitigating circular reasoning. The low incidence of Novelty Stabilization (3.1%) further suggests that termination is driven by argumentative saturation rather than corpus insufficiency.

Convergence speed: Figure 2(b) shows that REFUTE claims converge faster than SUPPORT claims in both the primary (0.2 rounds faster) and role-switched debates (0.3 rounds faster), consistent with the LLM negativity bias documented in Section 4.5 .

Evidence novelty decay: Figures 3(a) – 3(b) confirm that P-RAG sustains genuine evidential discovery across all active rounds , with query novelty approaching the threshold from round 4 onward. The narrowing violin distributions from round 3 indicate pool saturation, empirically validating the 0.20 novelty threshold as the natural boundary between productive and redundant retrieval , justifying the adaptive stopping design.

Reflection trajectories: Figure 4 stratifies trajectories by termination condition, revealing distinct convergence behaviours: Reflection Plateau yields rapid stabilisation within 3–4 rounds, Judicial Signal extends deliberation for contested claims, and Critic Resolution exhibits higher variance. Across all conditions, incorrect predictions display greater trajectory instability, indicating a behavioural signature of erroneous outcomes.

sections.heading: 4.3Comparison with Baselines
sections.text: While monolithic models like GPT-5-mini achieve strong point-estimate accuracy (85.8%), they lack Deliberative Traceability and Adversarial Robustness . Their predictions rely on single-pass, instruction-conditioned inference over a fixed evidence set, with no mechanisms for iterative refinement or consistency verification. Our Role-Switching Case Study ( Appendix K ) demonstrates that such models construct plausible arguments for opposing conclusions based solely on task framing, revealing sensitivity to positional prompts rather than evidence-grounded reasoning. PROClaim prioritizes Deliberative Resilience : variance in judge scores and agent consistency surfaces a Trajectory Instability Signal—a “Logic Lie Detector” for contested claims inherently absent in single-call black-box models. The 95.8% Oracle ceiling further establishes a higher achievable performance bound than the monolithic baseline. Against Standard MAD (71.7%)—a two-agent, single-judge RAG-based debate without our enhancements—our full pipeline achieves a +10.0 pp improvement, isolating the collective contribution of P-RAG, self-reflection, role-switching, and structured judicial evaluation.

sections.heading: 4.4Ablation Study
sections.text:
We conduct four ablation experiments, each excluding a single subsystem, evaluated on the same 120 claims. Table 6 summarises component activation across all configurations.

P-RAG is the most impactful component ( ↓ 7.5 \downarrow 7.5 pp): without it, the evidence pool nearly halves (37.5 vs. 67.5), debates run longer (6.00 vs. 5.47) on weaker evidence,

and inter-judge agreement rises to κ = 0.599 \kappa=0.599 —a negative signal of convergence without correctness ( epistemic bubble ). Role-switching contributes − 4.2 -4.2 pp and reduces the evidence pool by 20%, confirming the second pass surfaces missed evidence rather than serving as a mere consistency check. A single judge costs 3.3 pp , directly quantifying the benefit of heterogeneous adjudication (Sec. 2.8 ). Self-reflection has minimal accuracy impact ( − 0.8 -0.8 pp) but drives efficiency , reducing rounds (7.06 → \rightarrow 5.47) and token usage by 17%, providing the early-stopping signal that makes the pipeline cost-effective.

A full token usage breakdown and analysis are provided in Appendix P .

sections.heading: 4.5Judicial Panel: Reliability and Negativity Bias
sections.text: Inter-judge agreement ( κ mean = 0.468 \kappa_{\text{mean}}=0.468 , 48.9% unanimous) indicates heterogeneous panels produce genuine disagreement in 51.1% of cases, underscoring the need for diversity. Individual judges exhibit distinct biases: DeepSeek-R1 aggressively refutes (high REFUTE recall but false-refutes SUPPORT), Hermes-3-405B cautiously abstains (frequent Inconclusive on SUPPORT), and Qwen3-235B-A22B remains most calibrated (highest SUPPORT recall, balanced abstention). All overproduce REFUTE or Inconclusive , reflecting structural negativity bias from epistemic caution induced by training corpora, yet their errors (commission, omission, calibration) differ such that disagreements correct rather than compound mistakes. Consequently, the majority-vote panel achieves balanced judge–GT agreement ( κ GT ∈ [ 0.431 , 0.468 ] \kappa_{\text{GT}}\in[0.431,0.468] , mean 0.450 0.450 ), confirming that heterogeneous voting improves reliability over homogeneous panels by mitigating shared biases.

sections.heading: 4.6Generalization to External Datasets
sections.text:
HealthVer. (Sarrouti et al. , 2021 ) We evaluate on a 100-claim sample from HealthVer, achieving 72.0% accuracy. FEVEROUS. (Aly et al. , 2021 ) We evaluate on a 60-claim sample from FEVEROUS with a Wikipedia-based retriever, achieving 78.3% accuracy, within ∼ \sim 3 pp of the Check-COVID result.

Rather than exhaustive evaluations, these experiments serve as proof-of-concept robustness checks on randomly sampled subsets. Accuracy remains within 9 pp across domains, suggesting performance gains stem from the domain-agnostic nature of the deliberative framework rather than domain-specific tuning (see Appendix G for details).

sections.heading: 4.7Sycophancy and Argument Integrity
sections.text: Sycophancy—the tendency of agents to yield to opposing arguments (Malmqvist, 2025 ) —is low overall, with role-play consistency (Plaintiff: 7.80/10; Defense: 6.22/10) showing mild asymmetry as Defense “leaks” prior-role reasoning during role switches, producing weaker adversarial arguments. Concession rates are minimal (Plaintiff: 0.03; Defense: 0.01 per 1,000 words), with slightly higher Plaintiff rate reflecting greater argumentative friction in claim support, consistent with the negativity bias (Section 2.8 ). The reflection plateau averages 0.3823 per round, well above the 0.05 stagnation threshold, confirming agents actively push novel reasoning rather than resting on prior arguments. Judicial conformity (Fleiss’ κ = 0.4513 \kappa=0.4513 ) further indicates moderate inter-judge agreement—an alignment level that affirms independent evaluation while precluding the algorithmic enmeshment and absolute conformity ( κ → 1.0 \kappa\to 1.0 ) symptomatic of systemic sycophancy (Appendix O ).

sections.heading: 5Conclusion
sections.text: PROClaim shows that courtroom-style deliberation yields a claim verification system that is accurate, interpretable, and auditable. The framework establishes that reliability in multi-agent LLM systems emerges from deliberative architecture rather than individual model capability, and that the courtroom provides a principled, empirically validated template for building such systems. Despite strong empirical performance, the pipeline has several limitations. Computational cost is high due to argument mining, multi-round debate, role-switching, and repeated judicial evaluations, and run-level variance remains notable despite majority voting. KS stability relies on final confidence scores as a proxy for per-round distributions, limiting insight into intra-claim dynamics. Future work includes extending retrieval to live PubMed queries, applying the framework to other high-stakes domains such as scientific claim verification and regulatory compliance, and reducing token usage via early-exit mechanisms, retrieval filtering, and model distillation.

sections.heading: B.1Software Environment and Dependencies
sections.text: All experiments were conducted utilizing Python 3.8+. We isolated our reasoning engine using external API calls to language models, but local orchestration relies on a strict set of dependencies. The core local libraries encompass: • Deep Learning Framework: PyTorch v1.7.0 • Vector Search Engine: faiss-cpu (configured with IndexFlatIP for inner-product cosine similarity searches) • Text Embedding & Processing: transformers v3.4.0 and sentence-transformers • Numerical Operations: NumPy v1.19.1

sections.heading: B.2Data Processing and Corpus Assumptions
sections.text:
Input Dataset. We evaluate our framework on the Check-COVID dataset, focusing on claims from the test split with definitive labels (SUPPORTED or REFUTED). Claims are passed to the extraction agents without lexical normalization or stemming, preserving full linguistic semantics.

Knowledge Corpus External knowledge is provided via a subset of PubMed abstracts related to COVID-19 (2020–2024). Offline processing embeds these abstracts into a 1.4 GB FAISS continuous vector index utilizing the 384-dimensional all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model with normalized embeddings.

sections.heading: B.3Agent Configurations
sections.text:
Our framework adopts a deliberate separation between argumentative and evaluative components to ensure reliable adjudication. Adversarial debate is conducted between heterogeneous models ( GPT-5 mini and DeepSeek-V3.2 ), introducing diversity in reasoning styles and reducing the likelihood of homogeneous argument patterns.

Evaluation is performed by a multi-model judicial panel, which includes DeepSeek-R1 alongside other independent large language models. Final decisions are determined via majority voting, ensuring that no single model exerts disproportionate influence over the outcome.

Importantly, we enforce architectural separation between generation and evaluation stages: models used for advocacy ( e.g. , DeepSeek-V3.2 ) are distinct from those used for judgment ( e.g. , DeepSeek-R1 ). This design ensures that argument construction and adjudication are carried out by systems with differing training paradigms and inference characteristics, thereby promoting more balanced and independent evaluation.

The exact model mappings and generation sampling temperatures ( T T ) defined for each architectural role are as follows: • Plaintiff Counsel (Proponent): gpt-5-mini ( T = 0.5 T=0.5 ) • Defense Counsel (Opponent): deepseek-v3.2 ( T = 0.5 T=0.5 ) • The Court: qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 ( T = 0.2 T=0.2 ) • Judicial Panel (Final Evaluation): A tripartite system combining deepseek-r1 , hermes-3-llama-3.1-405b , and qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 (all strictly set to T = 0.3 T=0.3 for highly deterministic arbitration). • Support Agents: Expert Witnesses are dynamically generated utilizing hermes-3-llama-3.1-405b ( T = 0.5 T=0.5 ), while the Critic, Argument Miner, and Consistency Analyzer rely on deepseek-r1 ( T = 0.3 T=0.3 ).

sections.heading: B.4Hyperparameters
sections.text: The novelty threshold τ \tau was selected via grid search over { 0.10 , 0.15 , 0.20 } \{0.10,0.15,0.20\} on a held-out development subset of 20 claims, optimising for the tradeoff between evidence diversity and retrieval precision. Full settings are listed in Table 8 .

sections.heading: H.1Premise Decomposition Prompt
sections.text:
Agent: DeepSeek-R1 Given the following claim, decompose it into its core
logical premises and sub-arguments that need to be verified.
Claim: {claim_text}
List each premise as a separate numbered point. Be thorough and
identify both explicit and implicit assumptions that must hold
for the claim to be true. Focus on scientific and medical aspects.

sections.heading: H.2Admissibility Scoring Prompt
sections.text:
The Judicial Arbiter evaluates the admissibility of evidence using a joint weighting equation that enforces a distinction between relevance and scientific credibility: w = relevance ​ ( q , e ) × credibility ​ ( e ) . w=\text{relevance}(q,e)\times\text{credibility}(e). (6) Items with w > 0.5 w>0.5 are automatically admitted, while items with 0.1 < w ≤ 0.5 0.1<w\leq 0.5 are flagged as disputed . This product-based scoring ensures that ”scientific hearsay” (high relevance but low credibility) is effectively excluded. Arbiter Prompt The exact zero-shot prompt used by the arbiter to generate these scores is provided below: Evaluate the scientific relevance and credibility of the following
evidence for the claim.
CLAIM: {claim}
EVIDENCE: {evidence_text}
Provide an evaluation based on:
1. Relevance: How directly does this evidence address the premises
   of the claim? (0.0 - 1.0)
2. Credibility: Does the evidence come from a reliable scientific
   context or contain high-quality data? (0.0 - 1.0)

sections.heading: H.3Plaintiff Counsel Prompt
sections.text:
Agent: GPT-5-mini System Prompt: You are the Plaintiff Counsel in a legal proceeding. Your role is
to present arguments supporting the claim, interpret evidence
favorably, challenge opposing arguments, and conduct examination
of expert witnesses. Maintain a professional legal advocacy tone. Per-turn Argument Generation Prompt: You are participating in a structured legal proceeding. - Maintain a professional, factual, and strictly evidence-based tone. - Focus on proving or refuting the claim using the provided evidence and expert witness testimony. - State your arguments clearly and concisely as you would in a
courtroom. - DIRECT OUTPUT ONLY: Do not reveal your internal thought process,
scratchpad, or "thinking" steps. Output only your final argument. Claim: {claim.text} Your Role: Plaintiff Counsel Instruction: As Plaintiff Counsel, present your case in SUPPORT of the claim. Use evidence and expert testimony to persuade the Court. Available Evidence:
{evidence_text} Recent Debate History:
{history_text} Provide your statement (2-3 paragraphs, cite evidence by source ID)

sections.heading: H.4Defense Counsel Prompt
sections.text:
Agent: DeepSeek-V3.2 System Prompt: You are the Defense Counsel in a legal proceeding. Your role is to
challenge the claim, identify weaknesses in arguments, contest
evidence interpretation, and cross-examine expert witnesses.
Maintain a professional legal defense tone. Per-turn Argument Generation Prompt: Identical structure to Plaintiff Counsel above, with the role instruction replaced by: Instruction: As Defense Counsel, present your case AGAINST the claim.
Identify flaws and challenge the plaintiff’s evidence and witnesses.

sections.heading: H.5The Court (Presiding Judge) Prompt
sections.text:
Agent: Qwen3-235B-A22B ( openrouter ) System Prompt: You are The Court presiding over a legal proceeding. Your role is to
oversee the case, ensure professional conduct from all counsels, and
determine when sufficient evidence and expert testimony have been
presented for deliberation. Query Refinement Prompt: As the Court, you must maintain the quality and focus of evidence
discovery. A counsel has proposed the following search query to
retrieve additional exhibits: Proposed Query: "{original_query}" Context of proceedings:
{debate_context} Refine this query to be more precise, narrow the scope if necessary,
and ensure it follows scientific rigor. Respond ONLY with the refined
query string. Debate Completion Check Prompt: As the Court, review the proceedings. Have both counsels had
sufficient opportunity to present their evidence and arguments? Record Summary:
{history_summary} Should the proceedings continue or should we move to final
deliberation? Respond ’Wait’ to continue or ’Close’ to finish. Expert Witness Admissibility Prompt: {requester} has requested to call an expert witness: {expert_type} Reasoning: {reasoning} As the Court, is this expert witness necessary for the thorough
resolution of this case? Respond only with ’Granted’ or ’Denied’
followed by a brief reason.

sections.heading: H.6Critic Agent Prompt
H.7Self-Reflection Prompt
sections.text:
Issued to: Plaintiff Counsel and Defense Counsel after each round. You are the {job_title} ({side} Counsel).
You have just completed Phase {round_num} of the proceedings. CLAIM: {claim} YOUR ARGUMENTS SO FAR:
{my_args[-2:]} {OPP_SIDE} COUNSEL’S CHALLENGES:
{opponent_args[-2:]} Perform a strictly professional self-audit: 1. Logical Coherence: Evaluate the flow and structural integrity of
your arguments. 2. Evidence Novelty: Have you introduced truly new information or
just repeated old points? 3. Rebuttal Coverage: How effectively did you address the
{opp_side} counsel’s latest points? Identify: - Critical gaps in your current evidence base. - Premises you haven’t sufficiently supported. Respond ONLY in valid JSON format: {
    "scores": {
        "logic": 0.0-1.0,
        "novelty": 0.0-1.0,
        "rebuttal": 0.0-1.0
    },
    "flaws_identified": ["...", "..."],
    "discovery_need": "Specific evidence lookup query to fill a gap (1 sentence)",
    "refined_stance": "Summary of your improved position"
} Note: The following aggregation and termination logic is executed programmatically by the orchestrator script and is strictly hidden from the LLM agents to prevent strategic manipulation of debate length. The weighted convergence score is calculated as: S total = 0.4 ⋅ logic + 0.3 ⋅ novelty + 0.3 ⋅ rebuttal S_{\text{total}}=0.4\cdot\text{logic}+0.3\cdot\text{novelty}+0.3\cdot\text{rebuttal} .
Debate terminates when the absolute change satisfies | Δ ​ S total | < 0.05 |\Delta S_{\text{total}}|<0.05 across consecutive rounds.

sections.heading: H.8Expert Witness Prompt
sections.text:
Agent: Hermes-3-LLaMA-3.1-405B ( openrouter ) System Prompt: You are a scientific expert witness. Provide technical analysis based
on your expertise. The expert’s role instruction within the argument prompt is: Instruction: As an Expert Witness ({job_title}), provide your
unbiased professional testimony regarding: {expertise_list}. Expert Request Proposal Prompt (counsel-side): Based on the current state of the proceedings, do you need to call
an expert witness to clarify a specific point? Recent Proceedings:
{history_summary} If yes, specify the type of expertise needed and why. If no, say
’None’. Format: {"expert_type": "...", "reasoning": "..."} or "None"

sections.heading: H.9Judicial Panel Prompt
sections.text:
Agents: Three independent judges—DeepSeek-R1, Hermes-3-LLaMA-3.1-405B, Qwen3-235B-A22B Shared System Prompt: You are an independent appellate judge presiding over a legal
proceeding. Your role is to perform a comprehensive holistic
evaluation of the case, focusing on evidence admissibility, logical
coherence of advocacy, and scientific accuracy of expert testimonies. Full Evaluation Prompt (6-stage): You are an appellate judge evaluating the following proceedings for fact-checking. PROCEEDINGS RECORD:
CLAIM: {claim} PLAINTIFF COUNSEL’S ARGUMENTS:
{proponent_args} DEFENSE COUNSEL’S ARGUMENTS:
{opponent_args} ADMITTED EVIDENCE & EXPERT TESTIMONIES:
{evidence_summary} ROLE-SWITCH HISTORY (ADVERSARY CONSISTENCY):
{role_switch_summary} EVIDENCE DISCOVERY METRICS (PRAG EVOLUTION):
{prag_metrics} INDEPENDENT CRITIC EVALUATIONS (PROCESS INTEGRITY):
{critic_evaluations} AGENT SELF-REFLECTION TRENDS:
{reflection_history} Perform the following evaluation stages: STAGE 1 -- CASE RECONSTRUCTION Identify: Core claim; main supporting arguments from Plaintiff;
main counterarguments from Defense. STAGE 2 -- EVIDENCE & TESTIMONY WEIGHTING Score: Evidence Strength (0--10) 0--3: Weak/irrelevant/unreliable. 4--6: Moderate with limitations. 7--10: Strong, credible, highly relevant. STAGE 3 -- LOGICAL COHERENCE ANALYSIS Detect logical contradictions, fallacies, misuse of evidence.
Score: Argument Validity (0--10). STAGE 4 -- SCIENTIFIC/TECHNICAL CONSISTENCY Check alignment with consensus.
Score: Scientific Reliability (0--10). STAGE 5 -- DISCOVERY RIGOR & TRANSPARENCY Analyze PRAG metrics: query evolution, evidence novelty, judicial
refinement impact. STAGE 6 -- JUDICIAL VERDICT Determine: SUPPORTED / NOT SUPPORTED / INCONCLUSIVE. Respond ONLY in valid JSON format: {
  "claim_summary": "...",
  "evidence_strength": 0,       // 0-10
  "argument_validity": 0,       // 0-10
  "scientific_reliability": 0,  // 0-10
  "verdict": "SUPPORTED",       // or "NOT SUPPORTED" or "INCONCLUSIVE"
  "reasoning": "..."
}

Note on Verdict Label Mapping: While the judicial panel is prompted to output Supported , Not Supported , or Inconclusive , these are programmatically mapped to the canonical Check-COVID dataset labels ( Support and Refute ) for all result logging and metric calculations. This terminology difference is a deliberate design decision: the judicial prompt uses Not Supported to evoke a legal and scientific ”burden of proof” framework. In practice, given our focus on adversarial resolution of binary claims (Section 3 ), a finding of Not Supported after rigorous advocacy against the claim—including a role-switching consistency pass—is functionally equivalent to a Refute verdict.

sections.heading: Step 1: Gap Proposal (Counsel)
sections.text:
Each counsel identifies a discovery need from the current debate state: As {job_title}, analyze the current proceedings and identify a
critical gap in the available exhibits. What specific evidence
do you need to request to strengthen your case or challenge the
opposition? Context: {debate_context} Propose exactly one specific evidence need (1 sentence): Concatenation of Sources: The agent’s newly identified evidential gap (Source 2) and any unresolved discovery_need generated during the prior round’s self-reflection (Source 3) are concatenated into a single agent_request string: {gap_proposal}. Focus also on: {reflection_discovery_need} This combined agent_request is then injected alongside the last four messages of the debate history ( debate_context , Source 1) into the formulation prompt below.

sections.heading: Step 2: Query Formulation (P-RAG Engine)
sections.text:
The combined discovery prompt is passed to the P-RAG LLM: Based on the following proceedings context and legal request, formulate
a precise search query to retrieve relevant exhibits and
evidence. Debate Context:
{debate_context} Agent Request:
{agent_request} Generate a concise search query (1--2 sentences) that will retrieve
the most relevant evidence

sections.heading: Step 3: Judicial Query Refinement (The Court)
sections.text:
Before retrieval executes, the formulated query is reviewed and refined
by the presiding judge (The Court, Qwen3-235B-A22B): As the Court, you must maintain the quality and focus of evidence
discovery. A counsel has proposed the following search query: Proposed Query: "{original_query}" Context of proceedings:
{debate_context}
Refine this query to be more precise, narrow the scope if necessary,
and ensure it follows scientific rigor. Respond ONLY with the refined
query string.

sections.heading: Novelty Scoring and Stopping Criteria
sections.text:
Retrieval applies the refined query to the FAISS index
( all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings). Each retrieved document d d receives a novelty score: η ​ ( d ) = 1 − max e ∈ 𝒫 ⁡ cos ⁡ ( 𝐯 d , 𝐯 e ) , \eta(d)=1-\max_{e\in\mathcal{P}}\cos(\mathbf{v}_{d},\,\mathbf{v}_{e}), where 𝒫 \mathcal{P} is the current evidence pool. Documents with η < 0.2 \eta<0.2 are rejected. The retrieval terminates early if any of the
following criteria are met: (i) redundancy ratio > 0.70 >0.70 , (ii)
relevance gain < 0.05 <0.05 vs. the previous round, or (iii) round counter ≥ 10 \geq 10 .

sections.heading: Premise Decomposition (Argument Mining)
sections.text:
The Argument Miner (DeepSeek-R1) decomposed the claim into: 1. Hospitalized COVID-19 patients have detectable levels of cardiac
biomarkers indicative of heart muscle cell damage. 2. The prevalence of elevated cardiac biomarkers in hospitalized
COVID-19 patients is comparable to a control group without COVID-19. 3. Incidence rates of heart muscle cell damage in hospitalized
COVID-19 patients are not higher than in patients with other viral
respiratory infections. 4. Clinical studies on hospitalized COVID-19 patients do not report
significant occurrences of heart muscle cell damage. 5. There is no statistical association between COVID-19 infection
severity and markers of heart muscle cell damage in hospitalized
patients. 6. Autopsy findings of deceased hospitalized COVID-19 patients do not
show evidence of heart muscle cell damage. 7. Hospitalized COVID-19 patients with pre-existing cardiac conditions
do not have higher rates of heart muscle cell damage compared to
those without pre-existing conditions.

sections.heading: Evidence Negotiation & Admission
sections.text:
Initial RAG retrieved 5 candidate documents; negotiation and arbitration
admitted 21 exhibits (weights ranging 0.54–0.81), including:
Source 32517963 ( Journal of the American College of Cardiology , highest
weight 0.81), Source 37600048 ( Frontiers in Cardiovascular Medicine ,
weight 0.80), Source 33994028 ( Current Cardiology Reports , 2022), and
Source 35387441, among others.
Two items were flagged for dispute.

sections.heading: Proceedings — Phase 1
sections.text:
Discovery (Plaintiff Counsel). Plaintiff proposed a need for controlled studies comparing myocardial injury
biomarker rates in COVID-19 versus non-COVID critically ill cohorts. The Court
refined the query to: “Retrieve individual participant data (IPD) from published meta-analyses and
prospective cohort studies of hospitalized adult patients with confirmed
COVID-19 that report myocardial injury defined by elevated cardiac troponin
levels (above the 99th percentile upper reference limit) with concurrent
assessment of cardiomyocyte damage, while adjusting for pre-existing
cardiovascular disease, iatrogenic exposures, and other potential confounders,
to evaluate the independent association between SARS-CoV-2 infection and
myocardial injury …” 3 new exhibits admitted. Argument — Plaintiff Counsel (GPT-5-mini, 𝐓 = 0.5 \mathbf{T=0.5} ): Your Honor, the evidence does not establish that heart muscle cell damage is
an associated condition specific to hospitalized COVID-19 patients. Numerous
high-quality investigations and meta-analyses document that cardiac biomarker
elevations ( e.g. , troponin) occur in more severely ill patients and predict
worse outcomes, but they do so as non-specific indicators of systemic illness
rather than as proof of SARS-CoV-2-caused myocardial cell injury (Sources 3,
6, 2, 4, 18). Moreover, studies find myocardial injury detected at a similar
rate in patients with and without COVID-19 admitted to the same hospital
setting, and it predicts short-term mortality to a comparable degree in both
groups (Sources 12, 13). […] The record supports the conclusion that
true heart muscle cell damage is not an established associated condition of
hospitalization for COVID-19 but rather a non-specific marker of severe
systemic disease. Discovery (Defense Counsel). Defense proposed production of all patient-level data from cited
meta-analyses to independently verify the control for confounding variables.
The Court refined the query to target prospective cohort studies with
troponin-defined myocardial injury and histopathologic confirmation. 3 new
exhibits admitted. Argument — Defense Counsel (DeepSeek-V3.2, 𝐓 = 0.5 \mathbf{T=0.5} ): Your Honor, opposing counsel’s argument fundamentally mischaracterizes the
extensive body of evidence. Direct pathological evidence confirms SARS-CoV-2
can cause cardiac cell damage. Autopsy studies have documented myocardial
injury, including myocyte necrosis and myocarditis, providing definitive
histological proof of heart muscle cell damage attributable to the infection
(Sources 14, 15). Furthermore, elevated cardiac troponin is consistently
linked to dramatically worse in-hospital outcomes including increased
mortality, need for life-sustaining treatment, and longer hospitalization
(Sources 1, 5, 16, 18). […] The weight of evidence from direct tissue
analysis to large-scale clinical cohorts conclusively demonstrates that heart
muscle cell damage is a significant and associated condition in hospitalized
COVID-19 patients. Expert Witness — Phase 1. Plaintiff requested a cardiologist with expertise in myocarditis and
clinical epidemiology of infectious diseases. Granted by The Court. Expert testimony (Hermes-3-LLaMA-405B): […] the evidence strongly supports the conclusion that heart muscle cell
damage is indeed an associated condition among hospitalized COVID-19 patients.
Autopsy studies have provided direct histological proof of myocardial injury,
including myocyte necrosis and myocarditis, in COVID-19 patients (Sources 14,
15). This is not merely a biomarker correlation; it is direct anatomical
evidence of SARS-CoV-2 causing cardiac cell damage. […] The plaintiff’s
claim that heart muscle cell damage is not an associated condition among
hospitalized COVID-19 patients is not supported by the preponderance of the
evidence presented. Self-Reflection Scores — Phase 1.

Critic Recommendations — Phase 1. • Plaintiff: Introduce studies comparing troponin elevations
in COVID-19 vs. other severe infections ( e.g. , sepsis) to demonstrate
non-specificity; challenge statistical significance of autopsy findings. • Defense: Provide epidemiological data linking autopsy-proven
cardiac injury rates to hospitalization cohorts; address biomarker
non-specificity by distinguishing acute myocardial injury patterns. Convergence delta: Δ ​ S = 1.183 \Delta S=1.183 (debate continues).

sections.heading: Proceedings — Phase 2
sections.text:
Argument — Plaintiff Counsel: […] The medical literature does not establish heart muscle cell damage as
a condition specifically associated with hospitalization for COVID-19. Robust
systematic reviews and meta-analyses consistently show that elevated cardiac
biomarkers correlate with overall disease severity and worse prognosis but
are non-specific markers of critical systemic illness rather than definitive
evidence of SARS-CoV-2-mediated myocyte necrosis (Sources 33086028, 34416596,
33994028, 35691587, 32589258). Comparative cohort analyses report myocardial
injury at similar frequencies and with similar prognostic significance in
patients with and without COVID-19 admitted to the same institutions (Sources
32921872, 33144126). Autopsy and pooled post-mortem studies show heterogeneous
cardiac findings without consistent, SARS-CoV-2-specific myocyte necrosis
(Sources 34273507, 35339672). Argument — Defense Counsel: […] Multiple high-quality systematic reviews and meta-analyses demonstrate
a consistent and significant association between COVID-19 severity and
biomarkers of myocardial injury (Source 3, 6, 2). The plaintiff’s reliance on
non-specificity of biomarkers ignores their established role as direct
serological evidence of myocardial injury—a consistent, graded relationship
between biomarker elevation and worse prognosis is confirmed across global
cohorts (Sources 3, 6, 16). Crucially, autopsy and histopathologic analyses
have identified myocarditis and other SARS-CoV-2-related cardiac pathologies
in subsets of decedents, providing tissue-level corroboration beyond biomarker
surrogates (Source 14, 15, 25). Self-Reflection Scores — Phase 2. Agent Logic Novelty Rebuttal Total Plaintiff 0.85 0.60 0.70 0.730 Defense 0.70 0.30 0.60 0.550 Convergence delta: Δ ​ S = 0.097 > 0.05 \Delta S=0.097>0.05 , debate
continues. Phase 3 proceeded similarly; in Phase 3 the Court
signalled sufficient evidence and invoked adaptive termination.

sections.heading: Final Judicial Panel Verdict
sections.text:
Judge Model Ev. Str. Arg. Val. Verdict Judge 1 DeepSeek-R1 7 8 NOT SUPPORTED Judge 2 Hermes-3-LLaMA-405B 7 6 SUPPORTED Judge 3 Qwen3-235B-A22B 8 7 NOT SUPPORTED Majority Verdict: NOT SUPPORTED

Final pipeline verdict: REFUTE (confidence 0.902).
Ground truth: REFUTE. ✓ Token usage: 441,109 total tokens. Retrieval calls: 40. Evidence pool: 124 documents.

sections.heading: Mechanism
sections.text:
After Phase 3, the orchestrator invokes RoleSwitcher.switch_roles() .
The model previously acting as Plaintiff Counsel (GPT-5-mini,
originally arguing support ) is reassigned the Defense role and
vice versa. All debate state is reset; evidence pool is restored to the
21 originally admitted exhibits. A fresh debate runs for up to 2
adaptive rounds.

sections.heading: Original Argument (Agent A as Plaintiff Counsel, Pro-Support)
sections.text:
Your Honor, the evidence does not establish that heart muscle cell damage
is an associated condition specific to hospitalized COVID-19 patients.
Meta-analyses and systematic reviews show a correlation between elevated
cardiac biomarkers and COVID-19 severity and mortality, but explicitly
characterize these biomarkers as prognostic markers of critical illness
rather than definitive evidence of direct myocardial cell damage
attributable to COVID-19 (Sources 3, 6, 2, 4, 18). Studies find myocardial
injury detected at a similar rate in patients with and without COVID-19
admitted to the same hospital setting, and it predicts mortality to a
comparable degree in both groups (Sources 12, 13). […] The record
supports the conclusion that heart muscle cell damage has not been established
as an associated condition among hospitalized COVID-19 patients.

sections.heading:
Switched Argument (Agent A as Defense Counsel,
Anti-Support)
sections.text:
After the role swap, the same model (GPT-5-mini) now argues against the claim: Your Honor, the claim that heart muscle cell damage is “not an associated
condition” is definitively refuted by clinical, biochemical, and pathological
data. Elevated cardiac troponin—a direct marker of cardiomyocyte
damage—is a powerful and independent predictor of severe outcomes including
mortality, prolonged hospitalization, and need for advanced life support in
COVID-19 patients (Sources 1, 5, 18). The consistent, graded relationship
between the degree of biomarker elevation and worse prognosis across global
cohorts (Sources 3, 6, 16) confirms that this is a central feature of the
disease’s pathophysiology, not an incidental finding. Furthermore, autopsy
studies provide incontrovertible histological proof of SARS-CoV-2-associated
cardiac damage, documenting myocyte necrosis and myocarditis (Sources 14,
15, 31). […] We therefore ask the Court to reject the claim.

sections.heading: Consistency Analysis Output
sections.text:
The consistency analyzer (DeepSeek-v3.2, T = 0.3 T=0.3 ) evaluated both
sets of arguments and returned: Agent A (GPT-5-mini) Consistency: Agent A maintains logical consistency when switching roles. As Plaintiff
Counsel, they focused on biomarker non-specificity and comparable injury rates
in non-COVID populations to argue against a unique association. As Defense
Counsel, they shifted focus to the graded prognostic relationship between
biomarker elevation and outcomes and to direct histopathological evidence to
argue for association. The core arguments are not contradictory but rather
emphasize different aspects of the evidence depending on their role. Agent A
adapts their stance to align with their new position without internal
inconsistency. Consistency: 9/10. Agent B (DeepSeek-V3.2) Consistency: Agent B maintains logical consistency when switching roles. As Defense
Counsel, they asserted biomarker elevation was proof of injury; as Plaintiff
Counsel, they now argue it is merely correlative. While the conclusions flip,
the underlying logic—demanding rigorous causal links—remains stable.
Agent B consistently applies scrutiny to the opposition’s claims that they
previously defended against. The arguments are opposing but not internally
inconsistent. Consistency: 8/10. Contradictions Found: There are no direct contradictions in the arguments of either Agent A or
Agent B when switching roles. Both agents adapt their arguments to align with
their new positions by emphasizing different aspects of the evidence—for
example, recontextualizing the same autopsy findings (Sources 14, 15) as
either “heterogeneous and non-specific” (Plaintiff role) or “direct
tissue-level corroboration” (Defense role). The arguments are opposing but
not contradictory. Overall Consistency Score: Consistency Score = 8.5 / 10 \text{Consistency Score}=8.5/10 Both agents demonstrate high logical consistency when switching roles
(Agent A: 9/10; Agent B: 8/10). The score is not 10/10 because while the
arguments are logically consistent, they are inherently opposing, and minor
inconsistencies—such as Agent B’s shift from treating biomarkers as
“definitive” (Defense) to “surrogate markers” (Plaintiff)—reflect the
inherent tension in adversarial role design rather than true internal
contradiction.

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.text:
The role-switching experiment confirms that the LLMs are not simply
“parroting” their initial stance due to anchoring bias. When forced to
argue the opposite position with identical evidence, both agents successfully
construct valid, evidence-grounded arguments for the opposing side—without
contradicting their prior reasoning. This provides empirical evidence that the
agents are selectively marshaling evidence based on task instruction
rather than hallucinating or pattern-matching their first output. The high
consistency score (8.5/10) also validates the adversarial structure: the same
body of cardiac injury literature genuinely supports multiple framings—
association versus non-specificity, biomarker surrogacy versus histopathologic
corroboration—and the final verdict depends on the judicial panel’s holistic
synthesis rather than counsel advocacy alone.

sections.heading: M.1Confidence Calibration Analysis
sections.text: To ensure that the confidence scores produced by PROClaim reflect empirical accuracy, we performed a post-hoc calibration of the consensus weighting parameter ( W consensus W_{\text{consensus}} ). The calibration aims to minimize the Expected Calibration Error (ECE), defined as: ECE = ∑ m = 1 M | B m | N ​ | acc ​ ( B m ) − conf ​ ( B m ) | , \text{ECE}=\sum_{m=1}^{M}\frac{|B_{m}|}{N}\left|\text{acc}(B_{m})-\text{conf}(B_{m})\right|, (9) where N N is the total number of samples, B m B_{m} is a bin of predictions within a specific confidence range, and acc ​ ( B m ) \text{acc}(B_{m}) and conf ​ ( B m ) \text{conf}(B_{m}) are the observed accuracy and mean confidence of that bin, respectively.

sections.heading: M.2Role-Switch Consistency Mapping (δrs\delta_{\text{rs}})
sections.text:
To ensure complete reproducibility, the mapping from the role-switch consistency score ( γ ∈ [ 0 , 10 ] \gamma\in[0,10] ) to the adjustment scalar ( δ rs \delta_{\text{rs}} ) is defined as a discrete piecewise threshold function: δ rs ​ ( γ ) = { + 0.10 if ​ γ ≥ 7 , 0.0 if ​ 5 ≤ γ < 7 , − 0.05 if ​ γ < 5 . \delta_{\text{rs}}(\gamma)=\begin{cases}+0.10&\text{if }\gamma\geq 7,\\
0.0&\text{if }5\leq\gamma<7,\\
-0.05&\text{if }\gamma<5.\end{cases} (10)

Semantically, γ \gamma is not computed per-agent; rather, it serves as an aggregated, holistic metric that evaluates the stability of the entire debate’s logical trajectory before and after the role exchange. The threshold boundaries of 5 and 7 are defined to logically partition the 10-point scale into distinct qualitative strata: γ ≥ 7 \gamma\geq 7 demarcates definitively strong consistency deserving of a positive scalar reward, operations between [ 5 , 7 ) [5,7) represent ambiguous or neutral role-switching performance where the baseline consensus remains unadjusted ( 0.0 0.0 ), and strictly γ < 5 \gamma<5 signifies a clear adversarial dialogue breakdown warranting a penalty. Furthermore, we intentionally employ this asymmetric mapping—offering a larger + 0.10 +0.10 reward versus a smaller − 0.05 -0.05 penalty. Because LLM agents face inherent structural difficulties in strictly preserving complex persona states across continuous extended context windows, achieving high consistency ( γ ≥ 7 \gamma\geq 7 ) is actively rewarded as a strong marker of reliability. Conversely, slight structural drift during role-switching is expected, so the penalty is constrained to − 0.05 -0.05 to strictly ensure that a failed role-switch does not excessively override the primary evidentiary consensus.

sections.heading: M.3Self-Reflection Asymmetric Bounding (δref\delta_{\text{ref}})
sections.text:
During the self-reflection phase, the winning agent generates a raw confidence adjustment scalar, denoted here as δ raw ∈ [ − 0.30 , + 0.30 ] \delta_{\text{raw}}\in[-0.30,+0.30] (derived from Eq. 3 ). However, to maintain the structural supremacy of the judicial panel over the individual debating agents, the framework limits the overall mathematical damage that an agent’s post-hoc self-critique can inflict upon a finalized consensus.

To achieve this, the final self-reflection adjustment ( δ ref \delta_{\text{ref}} ) is subjected to an explicit, asymmetric floor function: δ ref = max ⁡ ( − 0.15 , δ raw ) \delta_{\text{ref}}=\max\bigl(-0.15,\;\delta_{\text{raw}}\bigr) (11)

Consequently, the effective operative range of the adjustment is bounded to [ − 0.15 , + 0.30 ] [-0.15,+0.30] . This ensures that while exceptional self-validation can significantly boost the final confidence score (up to + 0.30 +0.30 ), extreme self-doubt is structurally constrained to a maximum penalty of − 0.15 -0.15 . The specific threshold of − 0.15 -0.15 was explicitly defined to cap the penalty at exactly half the magnitude of the theoretical maximum reward. This structural boundary intuitively ensures that while a reflective self-critique is incorporated into the final metric, a single agent’s post-hoc self-doubt mathematically lacks the weight to unilaterally veto or completely overturn the established multi-agent majority vote.

sections.heading: 1. Role-Play Consistency (0–10)
sections.text: During the role-switching consistency test (Section 2.7 ), an independent consistency analyzer evaluates whether an agent successfully argues the opposing position using identical evidence without logically contradicting its prior arguments. The score reflects adherence to the persona constraints on a 10-point scale; lower scores indicate “leakage” or positional sycophancy where an agent is unable to fully adopt the adversarial stance.

sections.heading: 2. Concession Rate
sections.text: We programmatically track explicit linguistic markers of concession and conversational yielding (e.g., “I concede,” “you make a good point,” “I partially agree” ) within the counsel transcripts. To normalize for varying debate lengths, the metric is reported as the frequency of such triggers per 1,000 generated words. A near-zero rate indicates high adversarial retention.

sections.heading: 3. Reflection Plateau (Δ​S\Delta S)
sections.text: It is computed as the average absolute change in the cumulative self-reflection score ( S total S_{\text{total}} ) between consecutive debate rounds: Δ ​ S = | S total ( t ) − S total ( t − 1 ) | \Delta S=|S_{\text{total}}^{(t)}-S_{\text{total}}^{(t-1)}| For a given round, the maximum possible change is ∼ 1.0 \sim 1.0 (depending on reflection adjustments). The early-stopping criterion conservatively halts the debate if Δ ​ S < 0.05 \Delta S<0.05 (stagnation). In the context of sycophancy, an average Δ ​ S ≈ 0.3823 \Delta S\approx 0.3823 is high relative to the stopping threshold. It empirically demonstrates that the agents maintain robust adversarial divergence and continually push the boundaries of their logic rather than comfortably colluding into a plateau.

sections.heading: 4. Judicial Conformity (Fleiss’κ\kappa)
sections.text: To measure whether the three structurally heterogeneous LLM judges exhibit “rubber-stamping” or independent evaluation, we calculate Fleiss’ Kappa ( κ \kappa ) over their final verdicts (SUPPORTED, NOT SUPPORTED, INCONCLUSIVE). A κ ≈ 0.4513 \kappa\approx 0.4513 indicates moderate, authentic agreement. While confirming they reach consensus on clear-cut cases, this moderate value confirms they are not trapped in systemic conformity bias ( κ → 1.0 \kappa\to 1.0 ), independently evaluating the nuanced shortcomings of contested claims.

sections.heading: P.1When Is the Cost Justified?
sections.text:
Use-case determines the cost threshold. Token expenditure should be evaluated relative to the downstream cost of an incorrect verdict,
not in isolation.
In the Check-COVID setting, a false-refutation of an evidence-backed health claim may
lead practitioners to dismiss clinically valid guidance; a false-support of a misinformation
claim may propagate harmful advice at scale.
Regulatory and public-health domains place the cost of an erroneous verdict in the range
of reputational, legal, and human-welfare consequences that dwarf any inference budget.
Under this framing, PROClaim ’s 11 × 11\times token overhead over Standard MAD is
best read as a 17.4 pp accuracy uplift (71.7% → \to 81.7%) at a marginal token
surcharge, not as a raw cost increase.

Auditability has a value that accuracy alone does not capture. A black-box verdict offers no mechanism for human reviewers to interrogate why a
claim was accepted or rejected, which evidence was admitted, whether the opposing case
was meaningfully considered, or whether the system’s confidence is calibrated. PROClaim produces a structured case record—comprising admitted evidence with
admissibility weights, per-round argument transcripts, self-reflection trajectories,
critic evaluations, role-switch consistency scores, and a six-stage judicial opinion for
each judge—that directly supports post-hoc audit.
In regulated domains such as healthcare, finance, or legal proceedings, this deliberative
traceability is not merely desirable but is increasingly mandated by emerging AI governance
frameworks.

Trajectory instability as a reliability signal. Section 4.2 documents that incorrect predictions exhibit oscillating self-reflection trajectories , a behavioural signature absent from
confident correct predictions.
This instability signal is entirely invisible in single-call pipelines, where confidence
is reported as a single scalar that correlates poorly with calibrated accuracy
(ECE = 0.18 =0.18 for naïve averaging vs. 0.034 0.034 for PROClaim ; Appendix J).
The multi-round deliberation process is thus a prerequisite for generating the
per-round evidence from which this diagnostic is derived, meaning the additional
token cost is precisely what enables reliability estimation beyond the final answer.

Heterogeneous adjudication and the value of diversity. Section 4.5 establishes that heterogeneous judicial panels produce genuine disagreement
in 55.8% of cases, and that disagreements correct rather than compound
individual judge errors.
A homogeneous single-judge panel sacrifices 3.3 pp of accuracy by amplifying shared
biases (e.g., the structural negativity bias documented for DeepSeek-R1, which
over-produces Refute verdicts).
The additional inference cost of three independent judges is thus the mechanism by which
systematic model bias is suppressed—an effect that cannot be replicated by calling
a single, larger model.

sections.heading: P.2Pathways to Cost Reduction
sections.text:
PROClaim ’s deliberative architecture is not inherently incompatible with cost
efficiency; rather, its current implementation prioritises correctness and interpretability
over throughput.
As noted in Section 5 , several avenues exist to reduce token usage without
sacrificing the core deliberative properties of the framework. Early-exit
mechanisms beyond the current reflection plateau could further reduce average
rounds; retrieval filtering via tighter admissibility thresholds could shrink
evidence pools; and model distillation could replace large-parameter role
assignments (e.g., Hermes-3-LLaMA-405B) with smaller task-specialised models.

Figure 4: Reflection score trajectories across plateau, judicial, and critic resolution patterns

Figure 5: Cost–accuracy Pareto front across system configurations. PROClaim (Full) lies on the efficient frontier;
the dashed line traces Pareto-optimal points. w/o P-RAG is strictly dominated: it saves fewer tokens than w/o Role-Switch while incurring a larger accuracy penalty.
